formatted_summary:
agent2 (Round 2 summarized): The CSF findings—moderately increased protein, decreased glucose, and predominant lymphocytes—are characteristic of tuberculous meningitis, which also frequently involves lymphadenopathy in children. This pattern contrasts sharply with viral meningitis (lower protein, preserved glucose) and rules out bacterial/pyogenic causes given absent neutropenia and lack of severe inflammation markers.  
\boxed{D}

The CSF abnormalities match those seen in tuberculous meningitis—including moderately high protein, low glucose—and are consistent with lymphadenopathy observed clinically. These findings distinguish it from both viral meningitis and other infectious processes like cryptococtal disease or acute bacterial infections.  
\boxed{D}

Final summary:

The combination of mild-to-moderate protein increase, low glucose, and prominent lymphocyte count fits classical patterns of tuberculous meningitis, especially when compounded by concomitant lymphadenopathy common in pediatric cases. Unlike viral meningitis—which shows milder changes—or more aggressive forms involving bacteria/cryptococcus—the current data point most reliably towards tuberculosis-related CNS involvement.  
\boxed{D}. Answer choice: B
